---
title: "Job Market Analysis 2024"
format: html
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Introduction

This project examines business analytics, data science, and machine learning trends in the 2024 job market. The goal is to better understand what employers are asking for, which skills seem to matter most, and how students can position themselves more effectively for analytics-related careers. As employer expectations become more specific, labor market data can help show how technical skills, business knowledge, and workplace capabilities come together in real job postings.

# Research Rationale

This topic matters because students often hear that analytics and AI are strong career paths, but that advice is usually too broad to be actionable. A more useful approach is to study real job-posting data and identify the skills, salary patterns, and role characteristics that appear most often in the market. This helps translate general career advice into more practical guidance.

# Selected Topic

## Business Analytics, Data Science, and Machine Learning Trends

This project focuses on the following questions:

- What structured features best distinguish business analytics, data science, and machine learning roles from other job postings?
- Which job characteristics are most associated with higher salaries in analytics-related roles?
- Do natural clusters of analytics-related job postings align with occupational groupings such as SOC, ONET, or NAICS?
- What practical insights can students and job seekers use from these patterns?

# Methods Overview

The project uses a cleaned Lightcast job-posting dataset and applies several complementary methods. First, exploratory data analysis is used to summarize salary patterns, role distributions, work arrangements, and skill counts. Second, a skill gap analysis identifies the most common specialized, software, and common skills in analytics-related postings. Third, KMeans clustering is used to identify natural groupings of job postings. Finally, regression and classification models are used to study salary outcomes and distinguish analytics-related roles from other postings.

# Site Contents

This website is organized into the following sections:

- Introduction
- Data Cleaning & Exploration
- Exploratory Data Analysis
- Skill Gap Analysis
- Modeling Methods
- NLP Methods

# Preliminary Discussion

The results of this project are intended to support career planning by showing how labor market demand is reflected in real job-posting data. Rather than treating analytics careers as one broad category, the project separates skill patterns, salary relationships, and occupational structure to provide a more practical view of the market.

# Team Members

Carolina Resendez  
Saori Kikuchi
